summary: 52 tests cover the primary pipeline branches and structural invariants well. Four gaps prevent a 'passed' verdict: (1) the factory-state 'last-good non-stale cache' branch from the AC is not tested, (2) the replay-store retention-on-settle-failure TDD anchor (tasks/8.md) is absent, (3) the 402 body shape is never AJV-validated against the vendored x402 v1 schema (spec requirement, ajv is already a dependency), and (4) a verify-result field name mismatch between the mock and the switch creates a latent false-pass risk.

findings.title: Missing test: RPC error with non-stale cache returns last-good value (not settlement_failed)
findings.description: The tech-spec AC explicitly lists two distinct cache-failure modes: (a) RPC error with stale cache → 402 settlement_failed/rpc_5xx, (b) RPC error with non-stale cache → request proceeds using the cached value. Mode (a) is tested (line 298). Mode (b) is entirely absent. The `readFactoryState` implementation in core.ts does implement this path (lines 259-266 of core.ts: `if (cached !== undefined) return { entry: cached, servedStale: true }`), but no test exercises it. Without this test, a regression that deletes the stale-fallback path would go undetected.
findings.litmus_test: Delete the `if (cached !== undefined) return { entry: cached, servedStale: true }` block in core.ts → mode (a) test still passes (wrong), mode (b) test would fail (correct). Currently neither catches the regression.

fix.approach: Add a test in the factory-state cache group that: (1) makes one successful request to warm the cache, (2) advances time by less than 5000ms so cache is still fresh, (3) mocks readContract to reject, (4) makes a second request, (5) asserts the result is 'passthrough' (not 402). This proves the last-good non-stale path is alive.
fix.test_name: factory-state RPC error with non-stale cache returns last-good value and proceeds

findings.title: Missing TDD anchor: replay-store entry retained after settlement failure
findings.description: tasks/8.md specifies a mandatory TDD anchor: 'Replay-store retention on settlement failure: when settle returns failure, NonceStore entry for (from, nonce) is NOT deleted. Test asserts: simulate receipt_reverted → nonceStore.has({from, nonce}) === true after the failed call → a retry with the same nonce returns nonce_already_used, not a fresh settle attempt.' This test is entirely absent. The risk is real: if a regression removed the 'leave entry intact on failure' behavior from verify.ts/replay-store.ts, no test would catch it. The current mocking setup already provides __getNonceStoreForTests() to support exactly this assertion.
findings.litmus_test: This tests behavior the mock never simulates — verify is mocked in core.test.ts, so the NonceStore insertion is never exercised. The test must call the real verify path or use __getNonceStoreForTests() to directly probe the store state after a settle failure.

fix.approach: The test should: (1) use verify's real import path or call __getNonceStoreForTests() directly to check the store after a mocked settle failure; (2) mock settle to return { ok: false, reason: 'receipt_reverted' }; (3) assert __getNonceStoreForTests().has(from, nonce) returns true; (4) make a second request with identical X-PAYMENT and assert 402 nonce_already_used. NOTE: since verify is mocked in core.test.ts, the store is never actually populated by the mocked verify. This test may belong in verify.test.ts or replay-store.test.ts, but the tasks/8.md spec assigns it to core. Consider adding it to replay-store.test.ts and adding a cross-module note.
fix.test_name: replay-store entry is retained after settlement failure — retry with same nonce returns nonce_already_used

findings.title: 402 body shape is never validated against the vendored x402 v1 JSON Schema (AJV)
findings.description: The tech-spec Testing Strategy states: '402 body builder produces spec-compliant JSON (ajv-validated against vendored x402 v1 schema)'. The tasks/9.md scope also calls this out. ajv is declared as a dependency in package.json. However, no test file imports ajv or references a schema fixture. The no-X-PAYMENT-header test (line 152) checks individual fields via toMatchObject but never validates the complete body shape against the canonical schema. This means a deviation from the x402 v1 wire format (e.g., missing 'extra.assetTransferMethod', wrong 'scheme' casing, or an added forbidden field) would go undetected by tests.

fix.approach: Add an x402-v1.schema.json fixture to __tests__/fixtures/ (the vendored schema from github.com/coinbase/x402/specs). In the 'no X-PAYMENT header returns 402' test (or a dedicated body-shape test), compile the schema with AJV and validate result.body. Assert validate(result.body) === true. This catches wire-format regressions that toMatchObject misses.
fix.test_name: no X-PAYMENT header returns 402 body that validates against x402 v1 JSON Schema

findings.location: core.test.ts line 424 — verify error mapping parametrized test
findings.title: Verify mock returns { ok: false, reason } but VerifyResult type uses 'reason' — mapping test may silently pass even if core reads the wrong field
findings.description: The mocks in the parametrized verify-error test (line 424) return `{ ok: false, reason: verifyReason }`. The real VerifyResult type (verify.ts line 78) is `{ ok: false; reason: VerifyReason }`, so the field name matches. HOWEVER, core.ts accesses `verifyResult.reason` (line 474) via a switch — if core were changed to read `verifyResult.error` instead, all verify-error tests would still pass because the mock only returns `reason` and core's switch would hit no case (falling through silently). The test does not assert the 402 body's `error` field in the parametrized section — it only asserts the emitted event name. Adding an assertion on `result.body.error` for each verify reason would catch a core field-access regression.

fix.approach: In the parametrized 'verify returns X → core emits Y once' test (line 415-429), add: `expect(result).toMatchObject({ kind: '402', body: { error: verifyReason } })` after the emit assertion. This ties the test to the actual 402 response body, catching a regression where core reads a wrong field or the switch falls through.
fix.affected_test: verify error discriminant → D18 event mapping table (lines 415-430)

findings.title: Malformed base64 test is too permissive — accepts any of three phases
findings.description: The test at line 186 inputs `Buffer.from([0xff, 0xfe, 0xfd]).toString('base64')` and asserts phase matches `/^(base64|json|shape)$/` — any of three phases would pass. The comment says 'Buffer.from is permissive with malformed base64'. This creates ambiguity: if the phase classification regresses (e.g., 'base64' errors are re-classified as 'shape'), the test would still pass. A second separate test specifically triggering the 'json' phase already exists (line 208) and the 'shape' phase (line 221). The 'base64' phase test should use input that is NOT valid UTF-8 even after base64 decode, to lock in 'base64' phase specifically.

fix.approach: Change the assertion to `expect(logger.securityEvent).toHaveBeenCalledWith('malformed_header', { phase: 'base64' })` — matching exactly 'base64'. To guarantee this phase triggers, use a string that is invalid as a base64 alphabet character, not just content that decodes to invalid JSON. For instance: `'!!!not-base64!!!'` (contains chars outside base64 alphabet). Alternatively, keep the permissive test but document it explicitly as the catch-all for base64/json phase ambiguity.

findings.title: Missing test: vault address cached forever once non-zero
findings.description: The spec (tasks/8.md edge cases) states: 'vaults(developerEoa) once non-zero is final (factory deploys are immutable per D3). Cache forever once non-zero. Re-fetch only while still 0x0.' There is no test that advances the TTL past 5s and verifies that a non-zero vault address is NOT re-fetched from the RPC on the second request. The existing TTL refresh test (line 281) only verifies the 'paused' call count, not the vault lookup.

fix.approach: Add a test that: (1) makes a first request (vault returns VAULT_ADDR), (2) advances time by 6000ms, (3) makes a second request, (4) counts readContract calls with functionName === 'vaults', (5) asserts the vault lookup happened only once despite the TTL expiry.
fix.test_name: factory-state cache: non-zero vault address is NOT re-fetched after TTL expiry

findings.title: onSend hook in the last Fastify test initializes sentByPreHandler=false and never changes it — assertion is vacuously true
findings.description: The test at line 77 ('sets X-PAYMENT-RESPONSE header on passthrough without calling reply.send') adds an onSend hook that contains `if (reply.statusCode === 200 && payload !== undefined) sentByPreHandler = false;`. The variable starts as `false` and the hook only ever sets it to `false`. The assertion `expect(sentByPreHandler).toBe(false)` passes regardless of whether the preHandler called reply.send. The test proves the route handler ran via the 200 status and body checks, but the sentByPreHandler flag adds no coverage value.

fix.approach: Either remove the sentByPreHandler flag and its assertion (the test is already verifying the intended behavior via statusCode === 200 and body check), OR flip the logic: initialize sentByPreHandler = true and set it to false in the route handler's response to prove the route handler ran. The existing assertions on statusCode and body are sufficient to verify the intent.
fix.affected_test: sets X-PAYMENT-RESPONSE header on passthrough without calling reply.send (line 77)

branch_coverage.pipeline_7c_verify_all_7_reasons: covered (event mapping only, not body error field)

notes: core.test.ts mocks verify.js and settle.js via vi.mock at module level — correct isolation for orchestrator tests.
NetworkMismatchError class is preserved via importActual — correct, ensures instanceof works in core.ts catch block.
viem.createPublicClient and http are mocked — appropriate; prevents real network calls during unit tests.
keccak256 is preserved from real viem — correct; allows hash helper tests to exercise real cryptography.
adapter tests mock ../../core.js only — correct minimal isolation for adapter-layer tests.
No test mocks 3+ dependencies simultaneously in a single test — no Category 5 (excessive mocking) violations.
beforeEach resets all spies and restores default mock implementations — prevents shared state between tests.
Both adapters receive the same 402/passthrough test cases (4 tests each) covering the same input/output pairs — basic parity met.

Neither adapter test asserts that the same PaywallResult struct produces identical HTTP response fields across both adapters for the same input. This is not a blocking issue at the unit test level (that concern belongs to forked-e2e in Task 10), but worth noting.

fastify adapter is tested via real Fastify lifecycle (fastify.inject()) — higher fidelity than node-http which uses a hand-rolled mock ServerResponse. Both approaches are appropriate for their respective frameworks.

decision_matrix.rationale: high=2 and medium=2 meets 'high >= 1 AND medium >= 3' threshold for needs_improvement — just short of that exact condition (medium=2 not 3). However, high=2 alone triggers needs_improvement. The missing TDD anchors (T8-01 replay-store retention, T8-02 last-good-cache) are spec-required tests the orchestrator explicitly named. No critical issues and no tests hide bugs. Fixes are additive (new tests only) with no source changes required.
